title: Time, space and asymptotic growth

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Time, space and asymptotic growth; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Time, space and asymptotic growth
sources.claim: Asymptotic analysis describes growth in operations or space as input size increases using upper, lower, and tight bounds.

sources.locator: CS2023 Mathematical and Statistical Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Time, space and asymptotic growth
sources.claim: Asymptotic analysis describes growth in operations or space as input size increases using upper, lower, and tight bounds.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Asymptotic analysis describes growth in operations or space as input size increases using upper, lower, and tight bounds.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Two nested full n loops yield quadratic work while two consecutive n loops remain linear. Your goal is to derive and compare time and auxiliary-space bounds for iterative and nested algorithms. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf problem pseudocode; pf tools debug test; Correctness and counterexamples; pf control nested. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Asymptotic analysis describes growth in operations or space as input size increases using upper, lower, and tight bounds. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: Express the specification, invariant, proof, and asymptotic bound independently first; then implement in C with explicit bounds/ownership, C++ with value and library guarantees, Java with typed collections/equality, or Python with dynamic objects and library semantics.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Constants, input distribution, multiple parameters, amortization, and memory locality still matter for real constraints. The draft therefore rejects: Big-O gives the exact runtime in seconds for every machine and input.

includes: Count dominant operations and use O, Omega and Theta to compare growth independent of one machine.
Derive and compare time and auxiliary-space bounds for iterative and nested algorithms.

Express the specification, invariant, proof, and asymptotic bound independently first; then implement in C with explicit bounds/ownership, C++ with value and library guarantees, Java with typed collections/equality, or Python with dynamic objects and library semantics.
excludes: Constants, input distribution, multiple parameters, amortization, and memory locality still matter for real constraints.

outcomes: Derive and compare time and auxiliary-space bounds for iterative and nested algorithms.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Big-O gives the exact runtime in seconds for every machine and input.
misconceptions.correction: Big-O describes growth, not exact time; state the input measure, case, and relevant space separately.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Big-O gives the exact runtime in seconds for every machine and input.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Time, space and asymptotic growth, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.

limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
